content.problemStatement:
Given is a sequence AAA of length NNN.

Define f(i,j)f(i,j)f(i,j) to be the number of distinct elements in Ai,Ai+1,…,AjA_i,A_{i+1},\ldots,A_jAi​,Ai+1​,…,Aj​.

You need to process QQQ queries of the following two types:


1 x y1\ x\ y1 x y — set Ax=yA_x = yAx​=y.
2 k2\ k2 k — print ∑i=1k∑j=ikf(i,j)\sum\limits_{i=1}^{k}\sum\limits_{j=i}^{k}f(i,j)i=1∑k​j=i∑k​f(i,j).

Input Format

The first line of input contains two integers NNN and QQQ — the size of the array and the number of queries.
The second line of input contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1,A_2,\ldots,A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.
Then, QQQ lines follow, describing queries in the format given in the statement.


Output Format
For each type 222 query, print the answer to it on a new line.
constraints: The sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 2·10^5.
The sum of Q over all test cases does not exceed 2·10^5.
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case:
- The first line contains two integers N and Q — the length of the array and the number of queries.
- The second line contains N integers A1, A2, …, AN.
- Each of the next Q lines describes a query:
  * "1 x y" – set A[x] = y.
  * "2 k"   – output S(k) = Σ_{i=1}^{k} Σ_{j=i}^{k} f(i, j), where f(i, j) is the number of distinct elements in the sub‑array A[i…j].
All indices are 1‑based.
content.outputFormat: For each query of type 2, output the required value on its own line.

solution.approach:
For a fixed prefix length k, each position p (1 ≤ p ≤ k) contributes to S(k) exactly when the sub‑array contains p but does not contain any earlier occurrence of the same value. Let prev[p] be the index of the previous occurrence of A[p] (0 if none). Then the number of pairs (i, j) with i ≤ p ≤ j ≤ k and i > prev[p] equals (p - prev[p])·(k - p + 1). Hence
    S(k) = Σ_{p=1}^{k} (p - prev[p])·(k - p + 1).
Rewrite it as
    S(k) = (k+1)· Σ_{p=1}^{k} w[p]  –  Σ_{p=1}^{k} w[p]·(p-1),
where w[p] = p - prev[p].
If we maintain two Fenwick trees:
    FT1 stores w[p],
    FT2 stores w[p]·(p-1),
then S(k) can be answered in O(log N) as
    S(k) = (k+1)·FT1.prefix(k) - FT2.prefix(k).
Updates change the value at position x. Only the positions whose predecessor changes are affected: x itself, the next occurrence of the old value after x, and the next occurrence of the new value after x. Using an ordered set (std::set, TreeSet, or a treap) for each distinct value we can find predecessor and successor in O(log N). For each affected position p we recompute w[p] and update both Fenwick trees with the difference. All operations are O(log N), giving overall O((N+Q)·log N) time and O(N) memory.
Edge cases such as the first/last occurrence are handled by treating prev = 0, which makes w[p] = p.
solution.timeComplexity: O((N + Q) · log N) per test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for the array, two Fenwick trees and the ordered‑set containers.
